vulnerability.title: Disable core dump backtraces
vulnerability.description: The ProcessSizeMax option in [Coredump] section of /etc/systemd/coredump.conf or in a drop-in file under /etc/systemd/coredump.conf.d/ specifies the maximum size in bytes of a core which will be processed. Core dumps exceeding this size may be stored, but the backtrace will not be generated.
vulnerability.recommendation:
Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q systemd; }; then found=false # set value in all files if they contain section or key for f in $(echo -n "/etc/systemd/coredump.conf.d/complianceascode_hardening.conf /etc/systemd/coredump.conf.d/*.conf /etc/systemd/coredump.conf"); do if [ ! -e "$f" ]; then continue fi # find key in section and change value if grep -qzosP "[[:space:]]*\[Coredump\]([^
\[]*
+)+?[[:space:]]*ProcessSizeMax" "$f"; then if ! grep -qPz "ProcessSizeMax=0" "$f"; then sed -i "s/ProcessSizeMax[^(
)]*/ProcessSizeMax=0/" "$f" fi found=true # find section and add key = value to it elif grep -qs "[[:space:]]*\[Coredump\]" "$f"; then sed -i "/[[:space:]]*\[Coredump\]/a ProcessSizeMax=0" "$f" found=true fi done # if s

triage_decision.reason: Setting ProcessSizeMax=0 in /etc/systemd/coredump.conf or drop‑in files is a simple configuration change that does not affect authentication, networking, or critical services. It only limits the size of core dumps processed, preventing backtrace generation for large dumps, which is a standard hardening step with no risk of lockout or system breakage.

Use sed -i.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan sets ProcessSizeMax=0 in /etc/systemd/coredump.conf but does not consider existing drop‑in files in /etc/systemd/coredump.conf.d/ that may override this value. If such drop‑in files exist, the vulnerability would not be fully mitigated. The plan also does not verify that the section header exists correctly (e.g., missing whitespace handling) and uses a generic sed replace that could potentially alter unintended lines if the regexp is not precise. Additionally, reloading using systemctl daemon-reload is correct, but resetting the coredump.socket is unnecessary unless a service restart is required. Finally, the plan assumes the base file exists, which may not always be the case on minimal systems.
concerns: May miss drop‑in files that override ProcessSizeMax
Sed pattern may incorrectly replace unintended lines
Unclear if systemctl reset-failed is necessary
suggested_improvements: Check and update all files under /etc/systemd/coredump.conf.d/ that contain a [Coredump] section and set ProcessSizeMax=0; validate sed patterns to avoid accidental changes; use systemctl reload systemd-coredump.socket instead of resetting failed services; verify file existence and create standard template if missing; potential to test changes with systemd-analyze config-verify

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan correctly ensures ProcessSizeMax=0 is set in all relevant systemd core dump configuration files and reloads systemd, which will disable backtraces on large core dumps.
concerns: Replacement logic may affect lines outside the intended [Coredump] section if multiple sections appear in a file.
Adding a ProcessSizeMax line to every drop‑in could create duplicate zero entries, though harmless.
The plan reloads only the coredump socket; a full daemon reload or service restart may be preferable to guarantee the new configuration is applied immediately.
suggested_improvements: Limit the sed replacement pattern to only lines within the [Coredump] section (e.g., using a sed address range).
After processing all files, perform a single systemctl daemon-reload followed by systemctl restart systemd-coredump.service to ensure the new configuration takes effect.
Optional: run systemd-analyze config-verify before reloading to detect syntax errors.
When creating a new base file, include at least a header comment to document the purpose of ProcessSizeMax=0.
